seoTitle: Enter Form 1099-G in TurboTax the Right Way
metaDescription: Learn how to enter Form 1099-G in TurboTax to accurately report unemployment compensation or state tax refunds on your return.
intro: Form 1099-G reports government payments like unemployment compensation or state tax refunds, and entering it correctly keeps your return accurate. This guide walks through how to enter Form 1099-G in TurboTax, covering the common scenarios that trigger this form. It's aimed at anyone who received unemployment benefits or a state refund during the tax year.

steps.heading: Locate your Form 1099-G
steps.body: Find the 1099-G you received, either from your state unemployment office or state tax agency, before starting entry.

steps.heading: Access the relevant income section
steps.body: Within TurboTax, navigate to the section for reporting unemployment compensation or state refunds.

steps.heading: Enter the form details
steps.body: Input the amounts exactly as shown on your 1099-G, including any state or federal tax withheld.

steps.heading: Review for common errors
steps.body: Double-check that amounts match your form exactly, since mismatches can trigger IRS notices later.

steps.heading: Confirm the impact on your return
steps.body: Review how the entered 1099-G affects your total income and refund or amount owed before finalizing your return.

faq.question: Do I have to report a state tax refund shown on Form 1099-G?
faq.answer: It depends on whether you itemized deductions in the prior year — TurboTax will help determine if it's taxable.

faq.question: Is unemployment compensation always taxable?
faq.answer: Generally yes, unemployment compensation reported on Form 1099-G is taxable at the federal level in most cases.

faq.question: What if I never received my Form 1099-G?
faq.answer: You can typically retrieve it through your state's unemployment or tax agency website if it wasn't mailed to you.

faq.question: Can entering this form incorrectly delay my refund?
faq.answer: Yes, mismatched amounts compared to IRS records can trigger review delays, so accuracy is important.
